title: Southeast Asia & Oceania
subtitle: Temple cities, island stone, and the deepest-time painting tradition on earth.
intro: From the sandstone temple-mountains of Angkor and Borobudur to basalt moai on Easter Island and rock shelters in northern Australia where painting has gone on for 20,000 years.

works.title: Borobudur
works.desc: A stone mandala you walk. Climbing its nine terraces takes you through 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddhas to the empty stupa at the top.

works.title: Prambanan
works.desc: The Hindu counterpart to Borobudur a few miles away — a cluster of tall, sharp-edged temples to the Trimurti, walls carved with the Ramayana.

works.title: Angkor Wat
works.desc: The largest religious monument on earth. Built as a Hindu temple to Vishnu, converted to Buddhist use, and never fully abandoned.

works.title: Bagan
works.desc: More than 2,000 Buddhist temples and stupas on a plain by the Irrawaddy. At dawn hot air balloons drift over a landscape of brick towers.

works.title: Wat Phra Kaew
works.desc: The royal temple inside the Grand Palace, home to the Emerald Buddha — carved from a single block of jade, robed differently for each season.

works.title: Nan Madol
works.desc: A city of 92 artificial islets built from hexagonal basalt logs, laid across a lagoon. The "Venice of the Pacific" — and a place no one has fully explained.

works.title: Moai
works.desc: Nearly 900 stone figures, carved and moved miles from the quarry. They face inland — watching over the villages, not out to sea.

works.title: Asmat Carving
works.desc: Bisj poles and shields carved from mangrove with sharpened bone. Each pole commemorates a death and demands it be avenged.

works.title: Māori Wharenui
works.desc: A carved meeting house represents an ancestor's body — ridgepole as spine, rafters as ribs. Every panel tells a story in whakairo carving.

works.title: Tā Moko
works.desc: Tattoos chiseled (not punctured) into the skin, carrying genealogy and rank. Each design is unique to the person who wears it.

works.title: Ubirr Rock Art
works.meta: Aboriginal Australian · up to 20,000 years old · Kakadu
works.desc: One of the oldest continuous art traditions on earth. Later paintings show fish anatomy so precisely that species are still identifiable.

works.title: Mỹ Sơn
works.desc: Brick Hindu temples in a jungle valley. Seventy stood before American bombs in 1969; twenty survive, still slowly giving up their secrets.

works.title: Ayutthaya
works.desc: The Thai capital before the Burmese sacked it in 1767. Brick prangs lean at odd angles around the old moated island.

works.title: Sukhothai
works.desc: Serene standing Buddhas in the "walking" pose invented here — one hand raised, the other swinging at the hip, fabric flowing forward.

works.title: Wayang Kulit
works.desc: Flat leather shadow puppets of the Ramayana and Mahabharata, manipulated against a backlit screen. A traditional performance runs all night.

works.title: Đông Sơn Culture
works.desc: Bronze drums cast with spiral patterns and concentric bird-men. Traded across mainland Southeast Asia — a pre-Indic shared world.

works.title: Bayon
works.desc: The last great temple of Angkor — 200 serene faces carved into its towers, likely portraits of Jayavarman VII himself, watching from every direction.

works.title: Preah Vihear
works.desc: A cliff-top temple on the Thai border — 800 meters of causeway climbing to a sanctuary looking out over the plain. Thailand and Cambodia fought over its ownership in 2011.

works.title: Balinese Temples
works.desc: Every village has at least three: for the ancestors, for daily life, for the dead. Split gates, black palm-fiber roofs, statues wrapped in checkered cloth.

works.title: Indigenous Australian Art
works.desc: Bradshaw paintings (Gwion Gwion) in the Kimberley, possibly 20,000 years old — elongated figures painted with pigment that has bonded permanently with the rock surface.

works.title: Uluru
works.desc: A sandstone monolith rising 1,100 feet out of the center of the continent. For the Aṉangu it is the physical record of ancestral beings' creation journeys.

works.title: Angkor Thom
works.desc: The last capital of the Khmer Empire — nine square kilometers walled in laterite. At its heart, the Bayon; at its gates, giants pulling serpents in cosmic tug-of-war.

works.title: Ananda Temple
works.desc: A cruciform white temple with four standing Buddhas, each eighteen feet tall. From a distance the gold umbrella on its stupa is the last thing to disappear.

works.title: Besakih Temple
works.desc: The Mother Temple of Bali — 23 linked sanctuaries up the slope of Mount Agung. When Agung erupted in 1963, lava stopped meters from its gates.

works.title: Banaue Rice Terraces
works.desc: Stone-walled terraces climbing the mountainside. Built without wheeled carts, draft animals, or cement. If unwrapped they'd circle half the globe.

works.title: Plain of Jars
works.desc: Thousands of carved stone jars scattered across a plateau. Probably funerary; ground-truth research has been slow because the plain is still littered with unexploded American bombs.

works.title: Shwedagon Pagoda
works.desc: A gilded stupa 325 feet tall, covered in gold leaf and crowned with 4,000 diamonds. Said to enshrine hairs of the Buddha.

works.title: Banteay Srei
works.desc: "Citadel of Women" — a small temple of pink sandstone carved with almost impossibly fine reliefs. Malraux tried to steal four figures in 1923 and was arrested.

works.title: Tongkonan
works.desc: Ancestral houses with boat-shaped roofs soaring up at both ends. Each is built facing north, aligned to the cosmos of the Torajan dead.

works.title: Hei-tiki
works.desc: Nephrite pendants of a human figure worn around the neck, passed down through generations. Each is named; each gains mana from the people who wear it.

works.title: Rongorongo
works.desc: Glyphs carved onto wooden tablets — one of the few independent writing systems in human history. Still undeciphered; most tablets were burned by missionaries.

works.title: Candi Sukuh
works.desc: A Hindu temple built as a truncated stone pyramid — closer to Maya than to Angkor. Its reliefs are frank about fertility; the statues at the gate tell you what they're for.

works.title: Moon of Pejeng
works.meta: Dong Son tradition · c. 300 BCE · Bali
works.desc: The largest bronze kettledrum in the world — almost two meters across. In Balinese legend it's the fallen wheel of a chariot of the moon.

works.title: Wat Rong Khun (White Temple)
works.meta: Contemporary Thai Buddhist · Chalermchai Kositpipat · 1997 onward · Chiang Rai
works.desc: An entire temple sculpted white, glittering with shards of mirror. The artist intends it to take centuries to complete; hands rise from hell at the entrance.

works.title: Ban Chiang
works.desc: Painted red-on-buff pottery from a prehistoric village in northeast Thailand — swirling spirals and concentric whorls. Bronze was cast here earlier than the textbooks once allowed.

works.title: Mentawai Body Tattoo
works.desc: A Mentawai sikerei (shaman) in full body tattoo. Designs carry rank and history; the practice is said to be one of the oldest continuous tattoo traditions on earth.

works.title: Waka (Māori War Canoe)
works.desc: Large war canoes carved from a single tōtara trunk. Prows and sternposts carved with ancestors; the hull painted ochre. Rowed by eighty paddlers, two drummers, a chief at the stern.

works.title: Kris
works.desc: An asymmetric wavy dagger with nickel-iron pattern welding. Considered an heirloom with a spirit of its own — some can be named, inherited, or banned from leaving an island.

works.title: Thai Silk
works.desc: Hand-reeled mulberry silk dyed and woven into patterned sashes, sarongs, and court textiles. Nearly extinct by 1945; an American expatriate named Jim Thompson revived the industry before vanishing in Malaysia.

works.title: Hmong Textile
works.desc: Reverse-appliqué and cross-stitch panels in jewel colors — "flower cloth" (paj ntaub). Often incorporates figures and stories; since the 1970s used to document Hmong migration and war.

works.title: Batik
works.desc: Patterns drawn in hot wax on cotton, then dyed — the wax resisting the color. Javanese court batiks reserved some motifs for royalty on pain of death.

works.title: Wayang Golek
works.desc: Carved wooden rod puppets, fully dressed in miniature batik. Performed in all-night epics of the Ramayana and Mahabharata — the dalang puppeteer plays every voice.

works.title: Pura Tanah Lot
works.desc: A Hindu sea temple perched on a rock pounded by surf. At high tide it becomes an island; legend says sea snakes guard it from the base.

works.title: Tau Tau
works.desc: Carved wooden effigies of the dead, placed in cliff-face galleries above Toraja villages. Each tau tau faces outward and is redressed periodically by descendants; some are centuries old.

works.title: Polynesian Tiki
works.desc: Stone or wooden carvings of deified ancestors, dotting the Marquesas, Tahiti, and Hawaii. Variants spread as far as Easter Island's moai.

works.title: Malangan Carvings
works.meta: New Ireland · 19th c. onward · Papua New Guinea
works.desc: Elaborately painted wooden figures carved for a single memorial ceremony, then deliberately destroyed or allowed to rot. Their knowledge was owned as intellectual property; only certain carvers could make certain forms.

works.title: Wat Xieng Thong
works.desc: The royal temple of the Lao kingdom. Its sim has a sweeping tiered roof that nearly touches the ground; interior walls are covered in gilded-black lacquer tell-tales.

works.title: Bulul
works.desc: Carved wooden rice-granary guardians placed in pairs on sacks of grain and anointed annually with pig's blood and rice wine. Ifugao priests chant lineages of ancestors while invoking each Bulul; the more darkened with offerings, the more powerful.

works.title: Okir Carving
works.desc: Curvilinear vegetal ornament and geometric naga motifs carved into wooden house beams, brass betel boxes, and the okir frontispiece of the Torogan. The tradition survives Spanish and American colonial pressure as a distinct non-Christian Filipino art.

works.title: Torogan House
works.desc: An enormous elevated wooden house for the sultan's family, its floor held aloft by two-meter carved tree trunks whose projecting panolong prows evoke ships. Inside, one single gigantic room divides only by textile hangings and movable panels.

works.title: Vigan
works.meta: Spanish colonial · 16th–19th century · Ilocos Sur, Philippines
works.desc: A coastal trading town laid out on a grid before the 1572 Spanish conquest, blending Chinese, Ilocano, and Iberian elements into a distinct Philippine colonial vernacular. Calle Crisologo's cobbled streets and bahay-na-bato mansions survived Allied bombing because a Japanese general refused to burn it.

works.title: Paoay Church
works.desc: An 'earthquake Baroque' church with enormous coralline buttresses that curve outward like flying sleepers, bracing it against the region's seismic activity. Its bell tower stands detached in case of collapse.

works.title: Manunggul Jar
works.meta: Late Neolithic · c. 890–710 BCE · Palawan, Philippines
works.desc: A secondary burial jar whose lid is crowned by two figures in a soul boat: one dead, one paddling, arms crossed, eyes closed. Among the most important prehistoric objects in Southeast Asia, it appears on the Philippine 1,000-peso note.

works.title: Kūkaʻilimoku
works.desc: Feathered figures of the war god of Kamehameha I, their faces sheathed in tens of thousands of red and yellow bird feathers meticulously tied to a wicker armature. Grimacing pearl-shell eyes and dog-tooth mouths dramatize the god's ferocity.

works.title: Ahu and Moai of Easter Island
works.meta: Rapa Nui · c. 1250–1500 CE · Rapa Nui
works.desc: Ceremonial stone platforms on which the moai stood, facing inland over the village. A typical ahu runs 100 meters along the coast; some incorporate basalt pillars and red scoria pukao 'topknots' rolled from a separate quarry.

works.title: Marae
works.meta: Polynesian · pre-European contact · central and eastern Pacific
works.desc: Open-air rectangular ceremonial courtyards paved in coral rubble, with upright stone backrests for chiefs and priests. From Tahiti to Hawaii to Aotearoa, the marae was the meeting ground of cosmology, law, and genealogical recitation.

works.title: Tapa Cloth
works.meta: Polynesian bark cloth · pre-European to present · Fiji/Tonga/Samoa
works.desc: Non-woven fabric beaten from paper-mulberry bark and stamped, painted, or smoked with fine geometric patterns. Each region of Polynesia developed its own grammar; Fijian gatu vakaviti like this one are made for weddings and funeral exchanges.

works.title: Malanggan Carvings
works.meta: New Ireland · 19th–20th century · Papua New Guinea
works.desc: Painted wooden figures made for a single memorial ceremony and then deliberately allowed to rot or be sold off. European museums hold hundreds of examples; the meaning of each belonged solely to the man who commissioned it and died with the performance.

works.title: Abelam Baba Mask
works.meta: Abelam · 20th century · Sepik, Papua New Guinea
works.desc: Spirit masks used in yam-harvest ceremonies celebrating the long ceremonial yams that Abelam men cultivate for years. The masks top painted ceremonial houses up to thirty meters tall, decorated with rows of ancestor faces in red, black, and white.

works.title: Wat Arun
works.desc: The 'Temple of Dawn' across the Chao Phraya from Bangkok, its central prang encrusted with broken Chinese porcelain imported as ship ballast. Its five towers form a stone mandala of Mount Meru that Rama II rebuilt from a smaller Ayutthaya-era shrine.

works.title: Wat Pho
works.desc: Home of the 46-meter gold-leafed Reclining Buddha and Thailand's first public university, where medical texts were carved into marble plaques around the courtyard. Traditional Thai massage is still taught in an attached school founded in 1955.

works.title: Wat Phra That Doi Suthep
works.desc: A mountaintop temple reached by a staircase of 306 steps flanked by mosaic-tiled nagas. Legend says King Nu Naone chose the site by tying a Buddha relic to a white elephant and following where it fell; it collapsed here and died.

works.title: Shwezigon Pagoda
works.desc: A gilded cylindrical stupa that set the template for every later Burmese pagoda. King Anawrahta enshrined here the tooth of the Buddha brought from Sri Lanka — placed by an albino white elephant he released to choose the holy site.

works.title: Sukhothai Historical Park
works.desc: Capital of the first Thai kingdom, whose 'walking Buddhas' introduced a uniquely flowing and androgynous figural type. The open ruins of Wat Si Chum still frame a 15-meter seated Buddha peering out from a narrow gap between high walls.

works.title: Phra Pathom Chedi
works.meta: Mon → Rama IV · 3rd c. BCE core, 1850s outer · Thailand
works.desc: The tallest stupa in the world — 120 meters of orange-tiled bell. A Mon-era chedi encased in a 19th-century Siamese one, as if a building were its own tomb.

works.title: Preah Khan
works.desc: A temple-monastery and university at Angkor, its halls so extensive they once housed 100,000 people. Left partly jungle-swallowed — silk-cotton tree roots grip the stonework.

works.title: Thiên Mụ Pagoda
works.desc: Seven storeys of octagonal tower on the Perfume River. In 1963 a Thiên Mụ monk drove from here to Saigon and set himself on fire in protest — the photograph that shook a war.

works.title: Golden Rock
works.desc: A boulder entirely covered in gold leaf, perched on a cliff edge. Legend says it balances on a single strand of the Buddha's hair; male pilgrims apply new leaf, women may only look from below.

works.title: Imperial City of Huế
works.desc: The Vietnamese Forbidden City. Yellow-glazed gates, moats, throne halls, all pointed at the Perfume River. Much destroyed in the 1968 Tet Offensive; much slowly being rebuilt.

works.title: Hawaiian Feather Cloak
works.desc: An 'ahu 'ula — thousands of tiny red and yellow feathers, knotted one by one onto netting over years, worn only by the highest chiefs. The yellow feathers had to be plucked from a bird that has perhaps one or two per body.

works.title: Bisj Pole
works.desc: A mangrove tree stood on its head and carved into ancestral figures and phallic projections — raised at funeral feasts to demand vengeance for the dead. Left to rot after the rite.

works.title: Rai Stones
works.desc: Massive limestone discs up to 4 meters across, quarried in Palau and shipped hundreds of sea miles to Yap. Used as currency — but too heavy to move; their ownership history is simply remembered.

works.title: Banteay Chhmar
works.desc: A vast jungle-wrapped temple 150 km from Angkor, its bas-reliefs showing battles against the Cham and faces of Avalokiteshvara staring out of ruined towers.

works.title: Beng Mealea
works.desc: A temple in near-total collapse, largely untouched by restoration — stones thrown by fig-tree roots, cracks opening under the chapels. Angkor Wat as nature tried to reclaim it.

works.title: Ta Prohm
works.desc: The photogenic Angkor ruin, trees slowly pulling the stones apart. The French maintained it in the half-swallowed state in which they found it, as a contrast to the restored temples.

works.title: Phnom Rung
works.desc: A sandstone Khmer temple on an extinct volcano, aligned so the sun rises through all fifteen doorways on a few days each year. Once lost in jungle; restored in the 1980s from 8,000 numbered fragments.

works.title: Candi Mendut
works.desc: A Buddhist temple 3 km from Borobudur, squat under a great stepped roof. Inside, three monumental stone Buddhas — among the finest Javanese Buddhist sculptures surviving intact.

works.title: Po Nagar
works.desc: Cham brick towers on a hill above the Nha Trang river, dedicated to the mother-goddess Yan Po Nagar. Still active — Vietnamese and Cham Hindus both bring offerings.

works.title: Pura Ulun Danu Bratan
works.desc: A multi-tiered meru shrine built on a small island in Lake Bratan, dedicated to Dewi Danu, the water goddess. Appears to float when the lake fills.

works.title: Barong Mask
works.desc: A fanged lion-dog costume, body of woven raffia, danced by two men. Barong fights the witch-queen Rangda in an endlessly restaged cosmic battle — good versus evil, never concluded.

works.title: Rumah Gadang
works.desc: "Big houses" with buffalo-horn roofs rising to upswept points. Matrilineal — owned by the women of the clan, extended and re-roofed as generations add on.

works.title: Sepik Clan Mask
works.meta: Middle Sepik · c. 700 CE · Papua New Guinea
works.desc: A carbon-dated wooden mask 1,200 years older than European contact, showing the visual tradition of the Sepik was continuous long before colonial ethnography "discovered" it.

works.title: Abelam Yam Mask
works.desc: A basketry mask worn by the largest yam of the harvest — the Abelam rank yams by length (often three meters), dress them in individual regalia, and rank them in a nuanced hierarchy of masculinity.

works.title: Vanuatu Slit Drum
works.desc: A vertical log drum carved with a face, slit up the side, played with wooden mallets. Group drumming calls up ancestral spirits; solo drumming calls the village from the fields.

works.title: Nguzunguzu
works.desc: A carved black canoe prow with white shell inlay — a protective head lashed to the front of a war canoe, meant to ward off water-spirits that cause shipwreck.

works.title: Mahiole
works.desc: A feather helmet, bright red and yellow, crested like the mohawk of a Greek warrior — worn by royal chiefs with their 'ahu'ula feather cloak. Rembrandt Peale painted one seen in Philadelphia around 1800.

works.title: Marquesan Tiki
works.desc: Stocky ancestor figures of stone and wood, great round eyes, planted hands on rounded bellies. Polynesian ancestry made visible; the form Gauguin spent his last years sketching.

works.title: Cook Islands Staff God
works.desc: A long wooden staff carved with a head at one end and a series of human figures reading down the shaft, once wrapped in bark cloth and feathers — the condensed ancestry of a chiefly line.

works.title: Marshall Islands Stick Chart
works.desc: A lattice of palm ribs and shells describing ocean swells — not land maps but wave-maps, for navigating between atolls too small to see from far. Navigators memorized them, then left them behind.

works.title: Wandjina Rock Art
works.desc: White face with huge dark eyes and no mouth, rayed head like a halo. Wandjina are the creator spirits of the western Kimberley, repainted by traditional custodians over generations.

works.title: Tiwi Pukumani Poles
works.desc: Tall painted logs planted around a grave for the Pukumani death ceremony — ochre-painted, individually carved, left to weather and eventually return to the earth.

works.title: Papunya Tula Painting
works.desc: Acrylic on board by the painters of Papunya — Warlpiri, Anmatyerre, Luritja artists encoding Dreaming stories in dot patterns that read as maps, as abstractions, as secrets only partially revealed.

works.title: Buddha Head in Banyan Tree
works.desc: A stone Buddha head trapped in the roots of a banyan tree at Wat Mahathat — severed when the Burmese sacked Ayutthaya in 1767, slowly embraced by the tree over centuries. Icon of ruin and regrowth.

works.title: Cambodian Avalokiteshvara (Pre-Angkor)
works.desc: A standing sandstone bodhisattva from western Cambodia, pre-Angkor and pre-Hindu revival — one of the earliest Southeast Asian Buddhist sculptures, carved when Indic religions were freshly arrived.

works.title: Māori Kapeu
works.desc: A curved pounamu (greenstone) ear ornament, worn by chiefs and passed down through generations. The curved form inherits the moon, the unborn fern, and the fish-hook.

works.title: Dvaravati Buddha Bust
works.desc: A limestone bust with heavy lidded eyes — the Mon-Dvaravati style of central Thailand, one of Southeast Asia's earliest Buddhist sculptural traditions.

works.title: Ayutthaya Gold Buddha
works.desc: A seated Buddha cast in pure gold over a resin core — found in a crypt at Wat Ratchaburana. Sealed into the stupa wall in 1424, unsealed in 1957 by thieves, rescued by police.

works.title: Javanese Kala-Mukha
works.desc: "Face of Time" — a lion-demon face with bulging eyes and no lower jaw, carved over temple doorways to devour the evil that would pass through. Monstrous apotropaic face.

works.title: Majapahit Piggy Bank
works.desc: A terracotta pig with a slot on its back for coins — excavated from Majapahit sites in the hundreds. The word "celengan" (piggy bank) is still Indonesian for a child's coin savings.

works.title: Mahakala Door Guardian
works.desc: A stocky volcanic-stone guardian meant to flank a temple doorway, club raised, belly exposed in defiance. Mahakala guards a threshold still, in a gallery, against forgetfulness.

works.title: Prambanan Triad
works.desc: Three seated deities from the great Hindu temple complex of Loro Jonggrang at Prambanan — the brief Javanese reassertion of Shiva worship between waves of Buddhism.

works.title: Borobudur Buddha Head
works.desc: A volcanic-stone head once attached to one of Borobudur's 504 seated Buddhas, broken off and removed in the colonial period. Quiet, unflinching, the eyes half-closed.

works.title: Krishna Lifting Mount Govardhan (Phnom Da)
works.desc: A colossal sandstone Krishna from Phnom Da — arm raised to hold a mountain that is no longer there, its lost upper half preserved only in a second stele fragment.

works.title: Māori Hei-tiki
works.desc: A greenstone pendant in the form of a squatting ancestor, head tilted, hands on hips. Worn around the neck, passed down generations — each tiki accumulates the mana of its wearers.

works.title: Māori Pare (Door Lintel)
works.desc: A carved and painted wooden door lintel for a meeting house — three figures, spiral chests, tongues protruding. The pare marks the threshold between the everyday and the sacred inside.

works.title: Tongan 'Otua Fefine
works.desc: A small whale-ivory female deity figure — one of the rarest surviving Tongan carvings. Used in chiefly rites; most Tongan wood and ivory sculptures were destroyed at Christian conversion.

works.title: Javanese Bronze Deity (Female)
works.desc: A seated female deity holding a conch — part of the Ngandjuk bronze find, a cache of 94 ritual figures buried in Java when Mataram's power faltered.

works.title: Javanese Bronze Deity (Vajra)
works.desc: A male deity supporting a vajra on his finger — from the same Ngandjuk cache, showing the specifically Buddhist-Tantric side of late Mataram devotion.

works.title: Champa Heraldic Lion
works.desc: A sandstone lion from a Cham temple — paws and ears stylized, facing forward, closer to a Hindu Indian lion than to the actual animal. Cham architecture is Hinduized Vietnam.

works.title: Champa Apsara Bracket
works.desc: A sandstone apsara — a celestial dancer — once set as a bracket beneath a Cham temple roof. Her hip cocked, her breasts heavy, her expression far away.

works.title: Champa Enthroned Deity
works.desc: A seated stone planetary deity — one of nine graha — from a 9th-century Cham temple at Quang Binh. Indian astrological theology carved on the central Vietnamese coast.

works.title: Burmese Kammavacha
works.desc: An illuminated palm-leaf or lacquered manuscript containing the ordination text — the words a novice repeats to become a monk. Folios bound at top, dense with gold script.

works.title: Papua Spirit Board
works.meta: Papuan Gulf · early 20th c. · Papua New Guinea
works.desc: A flat wooden board (gope) incised with a clan ancestor face — hung in a men's house for protection. Each board had its own spirit, its own name.

works.title: Papuan Ancestral Figure
works.desc: A standing wooden ancestor with an exaggerated phallus, angular face, arms at the sides. Ancestral figures stood inside spirit houses to watch over clan descendants.

works.title: Sawankhalok Covered Jar
works.desc: Glazed stoneware from the Sawankhalok kilns — a pear-shaped jar with incised and underglaze-iron decoration, its lid a little pagoda. Sukhothai-era ceramics were exported across Southeast Asia.

works.title: Fijian Club
works.desc: A carved hardwood club for ceremonial and combat use — "ula" throwing clubs, "totokia" pineapple-headed clubs, each form named and categorized. Fijian clubs were family heirlooms.

works.title: Khmer Durga Slaying the Buffalo Demon
works.desc: Durga pressing her trident into the buffalo-demon Mahishasura — cast at Koh Ker, the sandstone capital Jayavarman IV built when he split from Angkor for seventeen years before the court returned.

works.title: Hevajra Bronze
works.desc: An eight-armed, four-headed dancing Hevajra — the esoteric Buddhist deity Jayavarman VII adopted when Angkor turned Mahayana in the twilight of the empire.

works.title: Angkor Wat Naga Palanquin Finial
works.meta: Khmer · Angkor Wat period · 12th c. · bronze
works.desc: A multi-headed cobra-king finial that crowned a palanquin pole — Vasuki coiling up to protect the canopy under which a noble rode through the temple courts.

works.title: Baphuon Kneeling Male Figure
works.desc: A bronze figure kneeling in añjali, the gesture of adoration — a portrait of a minister or king. Baphuon-period metalwork carried Khmer bronze to its height just before Angkor Wat.

works.title: Pyu Standing Buddha
works.desc: A small kaolin-clay Buddha made for a Pyu-period shrine in pre-Pagan Burma — before the Bamar conquest and the Theravada consolidation under King Anawrahta in the 11th century.

works.title: Mandaya Dagmay Ikat
works.meta: Mandaya · Mindanao · early 20th c. · hemp, warp-ikat
works.desc: A hemp dagmay cloth woven by the Mandaya of southern Mindanao — the warp-ikat patterns encode ancestor-spirit figures, and a woman's weaving skill shaped her marriage prospects.

works.title: Conch Shell with a Figure of Hevajra
works.meta: Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze

works.title: Head of a Follower of Shiva
Sheath for ritual conch shell
works.meta: Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor period · 1100s · shell and bronze

works.title: Ritual conch shell
works.meta: Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze

works.title: Khmer Vase
works.meta: Cambodia · 1100s · earthenware with dark brown glaze

works.title: Ritual conch shell base with dancing Hevajra
works.meta: Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze

works.title: Tripod stand with nagas for a ritual conch shell
works.meta: Cambodia, Khmer, Angkor Period, 12th-13th Century · 1100s · shell and bronze

works.title: One of a Pair of Decorated Palanquin Hooks and Rings
works.meta: Cambodia, Angkor Wat Style, 12th century · 1100–1175 · bronze

works.title: A Pair of Decorated Palanquin Hooks and Rings
works.meta: Cambodia, Angkor Wat Style, 12th century · 1100–1175 · bronze

works.title: Head of a Deity or a Deified King
works.meta: Cambodia, Koh Ker, Reign of Jayavarman IV, 928-941 · mid-900s · sandstone

works.title: Wall Hanging or Ceiling Canopy
works.meta: Cambodia, Khmer People, early 20th century · early 1900s · tabby weave, weft ikat; silk

works.title: Yakshini's Head
works.meta: Java, Eastern Javanese Period · c. 11th Century · terracotta

works.title: Goddess
works.meta: Indonesia, Central Java, Boyolali · 800s · volcanic stone

works.title: Padmapani
works.meta: Java, Pagan, Sailendra Period, 8th-9th Century · 700s-800s · copper

works.title: Waist Cloth (Kain Panjeng)
works.meta: Indonesia, Central Java, 19th century - early 20th century · 1800s-early 1900s · cotton: batik

works.title: Bodhisattva Padmapani
works.meta: Java, predating Nganjuk style, 10th century · 900s · bronze
